concepts.title: Definition and example of domain
concepts.conceptBody:
Domain

For a given function $y=f\left( x \right)$, the set of values which $x$ can take provided that for those values $\text{y}$ is well defined, is known as domain of the function. For example, $y=\frac{1}{x}$, here $\text{x}$ can take all real values except 0 because at $\text{x}=0$ the value of $\text{y}$ is invalid.

concepts.title: Domain and Range of Algebraic functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Algebraic functions

Functions consisting of finite number of terms involving powers and roots of independent

variable with the operations $+,-,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ },\div $ are called algebraic functions.

for example : $f\left( x \right)=x+\sqrt{x},\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+1},\frac{x+1}{x-1}$

1) Polynomial functions 

$\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\text{a}}_{0}}+{{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\ldots {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}$, where ${{\text{a}}_{0}},{{\text{a}}_{1}}\ldots {{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}\in \text{R}$ (i.e. real constants) and $\text{n}$ is

a non negative integer, is said to be a polynomial function of degree $n$ (given ${{a}_{n}}\neq 0$ )

for ex. $f\left( x \right)=3{{x}^{3}}+2{{x}^{2}}+x+1$ (polynomial of degree 3)

$f\left( x \right)=1$ (polynomial with degree 0)

$f\left( x \right)={{x}^{3}}+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-1}$ (not a polynomial function)

 

2) Constant Function 

If the range of a function f consists of only one number than $\text{f}$ is called a constant function i.e. $y=f\left( x \right)=a$ ex. $y=f\left( x \right)=1$

3) Identity Function 

The function $y=f\left( x \right)=x$ is known as identify function.


 

4) Rational function 

They are of the form $f\left( x \right)=\frac{P\left( x \right)}{Q\left( x \right)}\left( Q\left( x \right)\neq 0 \right)$

where $\text{P}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{Q}\left( \text{x} \right)$ are 2 polynomials in $\text{x}\And \text{Q}\left( \text{x} \right)\neq 0$ as it will make denominator 0 .

Domain : Here domain is all real no. excepts when denominator is zero [i.e. $\text{Q}\left( \text{x} \right)\neq 0$ ]

for eg. $f\left( x \right)=\frac{{{x}^{2}}-2x+1}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)}$

here domain $\in R-\left\{ 1,2 \right\}$ because at $1\And 2$ denominator becomes 0 .

5) Irrational functions 

Algebraic functions consisting of non integral rational powers of $\text{x}$ are known as irrational functions.

eg. $f\left( x \right)={{x}^{1/2}},\frac{{{x}^{1/2}}+{{x}^{1/3}}}{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-1}},\frac{{{x}^{2}}+1}{{{x}^{1/3}}-1}$

Tips for Algebraic functions 

  1. Denominator should not be zero.
  2. Expression under even root should not be negative.
  3. Odd roots of any real no is defined & at least one odd root of a real number is real.

Q) Find the domain of the function $y=\frac{x}{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-3x+2}}$ 

Solution : 

Given $\text{y}=\frac{\text{x}}{\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-3\text{x}+2}}$

for $y$ to be valid, the value under root has to be greater than zero (here it cannot be zero

because it is in denominator)

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{x}^{2}}-3x+2>0 \\ {} & {{x}^{2}}-2x-x+2>0 \\ {} & \left( x-2 \right)\left( x-1 \right)>0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & x\left\langle 1\And \text{x} \right\rangle 2 \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ domain }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\in \left( -\infty ,1 \right)\cup \left( 2,\infty \right) \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Domain and Range of Exponential and Logarithmic functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Exponential Function 

The function $\text{g}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\text{a}}^{\text{x}}},\text{a}>0,\text{a}\neq 1$ is said to be an exponential function.

It is divided into 2 parts depending on the value of a.

for $0<\text{a}<1$, y decreases as $\text{x}$ increases

a $>1$, y increases as $\text{x}$ increases

Shape of curve 

 

as we can see from the graph that the value of y approaches zero but is never 0 (i.e. asymptote) and can take all positive values.

Logarithmic Function 

The function $\text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{a}}}\text{x}$ is known as logarithmic function.

provided that

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & x>0 \\ {} & a>0 \\ \end{array}$

and $\text{a}\neq 1$

So the domain is very clear from the constraints only

Here also the function depends on the value of a. 

Properties of logarithmic functions : 

  1. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \text{ab} \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{a}+\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{b}$
  2. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \frac{\text{b}}{\text{a}} \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{b}-\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{a}$
  3. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}{{\text{a}}^{\text{m}}}=\text{mlo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{a}$
  4. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{a}}}\text{a}=1$
  5. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{b}}}\text{m}a=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{b}}}\text{a}$
  6. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{b}}a=\frac{1}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{a}}b}$
  7. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{b}}a=\frac{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{m}}a}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{m}}b}$
  8. ${{a}^{\text{log}{{a}^{b}}}}=b$
  9. ${{a}^{\text{log}{{b}^{c}}}}={{c}^{\text{log}{{b}^{a}}}}$
  10. If $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{a}}x>\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{a}}y\Rightarrow \left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} x>y, & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }a>1 \\ x<y, & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0<a<1 \\ \end{array} \right.$
  11. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{a}}}\text{x}=\text{y}\Rightarrow \text{x}={{\text{a}}^{\text{y}}}$
  12. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{a}}\text{x}>\text{y}\Rightarrow \left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{x}>{{\text{a}}^{\text{y}}}, & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a}>1 \\ \text{x}<{{\text{a}}^{\text{y}}}, & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0<\text{a}<1 \\ \end{array} \right.$
  13. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{a}}x<y\Rightarrow \left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} x<{{a}^{y}}, & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }a>1 \\ x>{{a}^{y}}, & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0<a<1 \\ \end{array} \right.$

Also, when we say $\text{logx}=\text{y}$, then we take log with base 10 .

Similarly for $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\text{x}=\text{y}$ we write it as $\text{lnx}=\text{y}$ (log with base e is also called natural $\text{log}$ )

Q) Find domain of $f\left( x \right)=\text{ln}\left( -2+3x-{{x}^{2}} \right)$ 

Solution : for $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be valid the $\text{log}$ function should be valid and for that $-{{\text{x}}^{2}}+3\text{x}-2>0$

 

Now, $-{{\text{x}}^{2}}+3\text{x}-2>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}-3\text{x}+2<0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}-2\text{x}-\text{x}+2<0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{x}-2 \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)<0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( 1,2 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ domain $\in \left( 1,2 \right)$

Q) Find the domain of ${{\text{e}}^{\frac{1}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-1}}}$ 

Solution : 

The function is valid for all real values except for those on which ${{x}^{2}}-1$ becomes zero.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore & {{\text{x}}^{2}}-1\neq 0 \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=-1,1,{{\text{x}}^{2}}-1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ zero }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \therefore & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ domain }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\in \text{R}-\left\{ -1,1 \right\} \\ \end{array}$

Q) Find the domain of the following functions : 
(a) $y=\sqrt{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \frac{5x-{{x}^{2}}}{4} \right)}$ 
(b) $y=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left\{ \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x} \right\}$ 

Solution : 

(a) Given, $y=\sqrt{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \frac{5x-{{x}^{2}}}{4} \right)}$

for this function to be valid, the term on R.H.S. has to valid. For that to be true there are 2

conditions i.e.

  1. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{4}>0$
  2. $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \frac{5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{4} \right)>0$

First solving for part 1

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{4}>0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}>0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}-5\text{x}<0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\left( \text{x}-5 \right)<0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( 0,5 \right) \\ \end{array}$

Now solving the second part

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \frac{5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{4} \right)\geq 0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \frac{5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{4}\geq {{10}^{0}} \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{4}\geq 1 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 5\text{x}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}\geq 4 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{\text{x}}^{2}}-5\text{x}+4\leq 0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & ~\left( \text{x}-4 \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\leq 0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{x}\in \left[ 1,4 \right] \\ \end{array}$

since both conditions have to be satisfied, we have to take the intersection of (i) & (ii)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ from (i) & (ii)

$\text{x}\in \left[ 1,4 \right]$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Domain $\in \left[ 1,4 \right]$

(b) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x} \right)$

for function to be valid

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x} \right)>0\And \text{x}>0 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x} \right)>{{10}^{0}}\And \text{x}>0 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x} \right)>1\And \text{x}>0 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x}>{{10}^{1}}\And \text{x}>0 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{x}>{{10}^{10}}\And \text{x}>0 \\ \end{array}$

combining both we get Domain $\in \left( {{10}^{10}},\infty \right)$

Q) Find the domain of the function : 

$f\left( x \right)=\frac{1}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( 1-x \right)}+\sqrt{x+1}$

Solution : 

This question is a mix of algebraic & logarithmic functions.

Now, $f\left( x \right)=\frac{1}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( 1-x \right)}+\sqrt{x+1}$

We will solve both the parts separately & then combine their results to get the final results.

$\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( 1-\text{x} \right)$ is valid when $\text{x}<1$

$\And \frac{1}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\left( 1-\text{x} \right)}$ is valid when $1-\text{x}\ne 1\left( \therefore \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{a}}}1=0 \right)\And \text{x}<1$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}<1$ except $\text{x}=0$ (from $1-\text{x}\ne 1$ because at $\text{x}=0$, denominator becomes 0 )

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( -\infty ,1 \right)-\left\{ 0 \right\}$ ………..(i)

now solving the algebraic part

for $\sqrt{\text{x}+1}$ to be valid $\text{x}+1\ge 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\ge -1$ ..………(ii)

combining (i) & (ii)

we get $\text{x}\in \left[ -1,0 \right)\cup \left( 0,1 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ The domain of the given functions is $\left[ -1,0 \right)\cup \left( 0,1 \right)$

concepts.title: Domain and Range of Inverse Trignometric functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Inverse trigonometric/inverse circular functions 

Functions involving inverse of trigonometric ratios are called inverse trigonometric or

inverse circular functions.

 

(a) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x$ 

Domain : $\left[ -1,1 \right]$

Range $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }:\left[ -\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right]$

Nature : odd ;

$y=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x$

 

(b) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}x$ 

Domain : $\left[ -1,1 \right]$

Range : $\left[ 0,\pi \right]$

Nature : neither even nor odd

 

(c) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=f\left( x \right)=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x$ 

Domain : $\left( -\infty ,\infty \right)$,

Range $:\left( -\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$

Nature : odd;

(d) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{cose}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x$ 

Domain : $\left( -\infty ,-1\left] \cup \right[1,\infty \right)$

Range $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }:\left[ -\frac{\pi }{2},0 \right)\cup \left( 0,\frac{\pi }{2} \right]$

Nature : odd; $y=\text{cose}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x$

 

(e) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x$ 

Domain : $\left( -\infty ,-1\left] \cup \right[1,\infty \right)$

Range $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }:\left[ 0,\frac{\pi }{2} \right)\cup \left( \frac{\pi }{2},\pi \right]$

Nature : neither even nor odd $\text{y}=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}\text{x}$

 

(f) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{co}{{\text{t}}^{-1}}x$ 

Domain : $\left( -\infty ,\infty \right)$,

Range $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }:\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( 0,\pi \right)$

Nature : neither even or odd $y=\text{co}{{\text{t}}^{-1}}x$

Q) Find the domain for the following : 
(a) $f\left( x \right)=\sqrt{\text{cos}\left( \text{sin}x \right)}$ (b) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\left( \frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2} \right) \right)$ 

Solution : 

(a) $f\left( x \right)=\sqrt{\text{cos}\left( \text{sin}x \right)}$ is defined if
value under root is non-negative
i.e. $\text{cos}\left( \text{sinx} \right)\geq 0$
but we know that $\text{sin}x$ lies between $-1\And 1$
$\Rightarrow -1\le \text{sinx}\leq 1$
$\And $ for $\left[ -1,1 \right]$ cosine function is always + ve
because $\text{cosx}\ge 0$ for $\left[ -\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right]\And \frac{\pi }{2}>1$
$\therefore \text{ cos}\left( \text{sinx} \right)\geq 0$ for all $\text{x}$
$\Rightarrow \text{ x}\in \text{R}$
$\therefore $ Domain $\in \text{R}$
(b) for function, $f\left( x \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\left( \frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2} \right) \right)$ to be valid
firstly $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}>0\And $
then $-1<\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}<1$
for the first part $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}>0\Rightarrow {{x}^{2}}>0$
because log can not be 0
$\Rightarrow \text{x}\in \text{R}-\left\{ 0 \right\}$ …………(i)
for second part $-1<\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}<1$
$\Rightarrow {{2}^{-1}}<\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}<{{2}^{1}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}<\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}<2$
$\Rightarrow 1\le {{\text{x}}^{2}}\leq 4$
$\Rightarrow -2\le \text{x}\le -1$ or $1\le \text{x}\leq 2$ ………..(ii)
though we can also solve the inequality taking cases & combing to get (ii)
from (i) & (ii)
$\text{x}\in \left[ -2,-1\left] \cup \right[1,2 \right]$

Q) Find the domain of the definition of function : 

(a) $f\left( x \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{sin}\left( x-3 \right)+\sqrt{16-{{x}^{2}}}$ 

(b) $y=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{2}{2+\text{sin}x} \right)$ 

Solution : 

(a) Given $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{sin}\left( \text{x}-3 \right)+\sqrt{16-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$

for $f\left( x \right)$ to be well defined

$\text{sin}\left( x-3 \right)>0$

& we know $\text{sinx}$ is positive in $\left( 0,\pi \right)$. So generalizing it, $\text{sinx}$ will be positive in

$(2\text{n}\pi +0$, $2n\pi +\pi )$ as $2\pi $ is the period of $\text{sin}x$.

$\Rightarrow 2\text{n}\pi +0<\left( \text{x}-3 \right)<2\text{n}\pi +\pi $

$\Rightarrow 2\text{n}\pi +3<\text{x}<\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\pi +3$

for the under root part

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 16-{{\text{x}}^{2}}\geq 0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{x}^{2}}-16\leq 0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{x}-4 \right)\left( \text{x}+4 \right)\leq 0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left[ -4,4 \right] \\ \end{array}$

combining (i) & (ii)

from (i) $\text{x}\in (2\text{n}\pi +3,\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\pi $ where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2$

(ii) $\text{x}\in \left[ -4,4 \right]$

$\therefore$ The common region is $\left( -2\pi +3,-\pi +3 \right)\cup \left( 3,4 \right]$
$\therefore$ Domain is $\left( -2\pi +3,-\pi +3 \right)\cup \left( 3,4 \right]$

(b) given $y=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{2}{2+\text{sin}x} \right)$
for $\text{y}$ to be defined.
$-1\le \frac{2}{2+\text{sin}x}\leq 1$
solving first $-1\le \frac{2}{2+\text{sin}x}$
denominator here can never be negative or zero because $\text{sin}x\in \left[ -1,1 \right]$, cross multiplying
$\therefore -\left( 2+\text{sinx} \right)\leq 2$
$\Rightarrow -2-\text{sinx}\leq 2$
$\Rightarrow \text{ sinx}\geq -3$
which is true for all values of $\text{x}$ as $\text{min}$. value of $\text{sinx}$ is -1. now solving the second part
$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \frac{2}{2+\text{sinx}}\leq 1 \\ \Rightarrow & 2\leq 2+\text{sinx} \\ \Rightarrow  & \text{sinx}\geq 0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2\text{n}\pi +0\leq \text{x}\leq 2\text{n}\pi +\pi \\ \Rightarrow & 2\text{n}\pi \leq \text{x}\leq \left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\pi \\ \therefore & \text{combining }\left( \text{i} \right)~\And~ \left( \text{ii} \right) \\ \end{array}$
Domain $\in \left( 2\text{n}\pi ,\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\pi \right)$

concepts.title: Domain and Range of Modulus function
concepts.conceptBody:
Some other functions 

Absolute Value / Modulus function 

By absolute / modulus function we mean only the numerical value of the function, irrespective of its sign, from origin.

This concept is analogous to distance. We can also say that modulus function is distance with respect to origin.

Though, modulus function is defined as $\text{f}:\text{R}\to \text{R},\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left| \text{x} \right|$ here,

The $\left| \text{x} \right|$ can be defined as follows: $\left| \text{x} \right|$

$\left| x \right|=\left\{ \begin{matrix} -x & ,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }x<0 \\ x & ,x\geq 0 \\ \end{matrix} \right\}$

for ex. $\left| -1 \right|=1,\left| 2.8 \right|=2.8,\left| -7.9 \right|=7.9$

here we can understand this as distance, for ex. take $\mid -7.9$ |

On number line the distance of -7.9 from origin is 7.9 . So the value of modulus function is 7.9 So if you are given

Basic properties of $\left| x \right|$

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \left| x \right| \right|=\left| x \right|$
  • $\left| xy \right|=\left| x \right|\left| y \right|$
  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \frac{\text{x}}{\text{y}} \right|=\frac{\left| \text{x} \right|}{\left| \text{y} \right|},\text{y}\ne 0$
  • $\left| x+y \right|\le \left| x \right|+\left| y \right|$
  • $\left| x-y \right|\ge \left| x \right|-\left| y \right|$

last two properties are intersecting ones, you can prove them by putting values.

Q) Find the domain of the following function : 
(a) $\left| \frac{2}{x-4} \right|>1$ (b) $\frac{\left| \text{x} \right|-1}{\left| \text{x} \right|-2}\ge 0$ 
(c) $\left| x-1\left| + \right|x-2 \right|\ge 4$ (d) $\frac{\left| \text{x}+3 \right|+\text{x}}{\text{x}+2}>1$

Solution : 

(a) We have $\left| \frac{2}{x-4} \right|>1$

we can see that $x\ne 4$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{2}{\left| x-4 \right|}>1$ $\left\{ \because \left| \frac{\text{a}}{\text{b}} \right|=\frac{\left| \text{a} \right|}{\left| \text{b} \right|}\And \left| 2 \right|=2 \right\}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2>\left| \text{x}-4 \right|$ {we can do this because mod function is always positive}

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \text{x}-4 \right|<2$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-2<\text{x}-4<2$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2<\text{x}<6$

$\text{x}\in \left( 2,6 \right)-\left\{ 4 \right\}$ (Note : Remember to remove 4 from domain students generally miss this step)

 

(b) we have, $\frac{\left| \text{x} \right|-1}{\left| \text{x} \right|-2}\ge 0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ let }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \text{x} \right|=\text{y} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \frac{\text{y}-1}{\text{y}-2}\ge 0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{y}>2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}\leq 1 \\ \end{array}$

Note : we cannot include $\text{y}=2$.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \text{x} \right|>2$ or $\left| \text{x} \right|\le 1$

$(\text{x}>2$ or $\text{x}<-2)$ or $\left( -1\le \text{x}\le 1 \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Domain $\in \left( -\infty ,-2 \right)\cup \left[ -1,1 \right]\cup \left( 2,\infty \right)$

(c) we have $\left| x-1\left| + \right|x-2 \right|\ge 4$

we will solve this by finding the critical points $\And $ checking for values greater or smaller about these critical points. Here critical points are $1\And 2$

$\left| x-1 \right|=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \left( x-1 \right) & ,x\geq 1 \\ -\left( x-1 \right) & ,x<1 \\ \end{array} \right\}$

$\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| x-2 \right|=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \left( x-2 \right), & x\geq 2 \\ -\left( x-2 \right), & x<2 \\ \end{array} \right\}$

we can divide the values in 3 region i.e. $<1$, between $1\And 2,\And $ greater than 2 .

Case 1: when $-\infty <\text{x}<1$

i.e. in this region $\left| \text{x}-1 \right|=-\left( \text{x}-1 \right)$

$\And \left| \text{x}-2 \right|=-\left( \text{x}-2 \right)$

$\left| \text{x}-1\left| + \right|\text{x}-2 \right|\ge 4$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\left( \text{x}-1 \right)-\left( \text{x}-2 \right)\ge 4$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-2\text{x}+3\ge 4$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{x}-1\le 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\le -1/2$ ………..(i).

Case 2 : when $1\le \text{x}\le 2$

here in this region $\left| \text{x}-1 \right|=\text{x}+1$

$\And \left| x-2 \right|=-\left( x-2 \right)$

$\left| x-1\left| + \right|x-2 \right|\ge 4$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}-1-\left( \text{x}-2 \right)\ge 4$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }1\ge 4$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ no solution for this solution ………..(ii)

Case 3 : when $\text{x}>2$

here both are positive i.e. $\left| \text{x}-1 \right|=\text{x}-1$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \And \left| x-2 \right|=x-2 \\ {} & ~\left| x-1\left| + \right|x-2 \right|\geq 4 \\ \Rightarrow & x-1+x-2\geq 4 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 2x-3\geq 4 \\ \Rightarrow & x\geq 7/2 \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ combining (i), (ii) & (iii) ………….(iii)

Domain $\in \left( -\infty ,\frac{-1}{2} \right]\cup \left[ \frac{7}{2},\infty \right)$

(d) $\frac{\left| \text{x}+3 \right|+\text{x}}{\text{x}+2}>1$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\left| \text{x}+3 \right|+\text{x}}{\text{x}+2}-1>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\left| \text{x}+3 \right|+\text{x}-\left( \text{x}+2 \right)}{\text{x}+2}>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\left| \text{x}+3 \right|-2}{\text{x}+2}>0$ ………….(i)

we know that $\text{x}\ne \left( -2 \right)$

now 2 cases arise for $\left| x+3 \right|$

Case 1: $\text{x}+3<0$

 

$\left| \text{x}+3 \right|=-\left( \text{x}-3 \right)$

putting these values in (i)

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{-\left( \text{x}+3 \right)-2}{\text{x}+2}>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{-x-5}{x+2}>0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{x}+5}{\text{x}+2}<0$

[Now here note that we are getting answer $\left( -5,-2 \right)$ but do not forget that this case is for $\text{x}<-3$].

So the answer for this part is $\left( -5,-3 \right]$ …………(ii)

Case : $x+3\geq 0$, for $x\geq -3$

 

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \text{x}+3 \right|=\text{x}+3$

i.e. $\frac{\left( x+3 \right)-2}{x+2}\geq 0$

$\frac{\text{x}+1}{\text{x}+2}\geq 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( -\infty ,-2\left] \cup \right[-1,\infty \right)$

but again this region is for $\text{x}\geq -3$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left[ -3,-2 \right)\cup \left[ -1,\infty \right)$ ………..(iii)

combining (ii) & (iii)

Domain $\in \left( -5,-3 \right)\cup \left[ -3,-2 \right)\cup \left[ -1,\infty \right)$

$\in \left[ -5,-2 \right)\cup \left[ -1,\infty \right)$

concepts.title: Domain and Range of Greatest Integer function and Signum function
concepts.conceptBody:
Greatest Integer function/Step function 

The function $\text{y}=\left[ \text{x} \right]$ is known as greatest Integer function & is defined as greatest

integer less than equal to $\text{x}$.

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\left[ x \right]=a$ if $a\le x<a+1$

for example

$\left[ 2.3\left] =2, \right[5.9\left] =5, \right[7 \right]=7,$

take special care of negative values

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \left[ -7.9\left] =-8, \right[-5 \right]=-5 \\ {} & y=f\left( x \right)=\left[ x \right] \\ \end{array}$

The function is called step function as we can see from the graph that it follows a step like curve

Fraction part of x : 

 

for example if $\text{x}=5.9$ then fractional part of $\text{x}$ is .9

It is denoted by $\left\{ \text{x} \right\}$

Thus, $\left\{ \text{x} \right\}=\text{x}-\left[ \text{x} \right]$ & $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0\le \left\{ \text{x} \right\}<1$

$y=f\left( x \right)=\left\{ x \right\}$

Some properties of greatest integer function & fractional part 

(i) $\left[ \left[ \text{x} \right] \right]=\left[ \text{x} \right]$

(ii) $\left[ \text{x}+\text{n} \right]=\left[ \text{x} \right]+\text{n}$, if $\text{n}$ is an integer

(iii) $\left[ \left\{ \text{x} \right\} \right]=0,\left\{ \left[ \text{x} \right] \right\}=0$

(iv) $\left[ \text{x} \right]+\left[ -\text{x} \right]=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ -1;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\notin \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array} \right\}$

(v) $\left\{ \text{x} \right\}+\left\{ -\text{x} \right\}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 & ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ +1 & ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\notin \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array} \right\}$ (vi) $\left[ -\text{x} \right]=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} -\left[ \text{x} \right]\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ; }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ -\left[ \text{x} \right]-1;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\notin \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array} \right\}$

(vii) $\left[ \text{x}+\text{y} \right]=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \left[ \text{x} \right]+\left[ \text{y} \right], & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ either }\!\!~\!\!\text{ one }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & \cdot \left\{ \text{x} \right\}+\left\{ \text{y} \right\}<1 \\ \left[ \text{x} \right]+\left[ \text{y} \right]+1 & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ when }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{x} \right\}+\left\{ \text{y} \right\}\ge 1 \\ \end{array} \right.$

(viii) $\left[ \frac{\left[ \text{x} \right]}{\text{n}} \right]=\left[ \frac{\text{x}}{\text{n}} \right],\text{n}\in \text{N}$

(ix) $\left[ \text{x} \right]+\left[ \text{x}+\frac{1}{\text{n}} \right]+\left[ \text{x}+\frac{2}{\text{n}} \right]+\ldots \ldots +\left[ \text{x}+\frac{\text{n}-1}{\text{n}} \right]=\left[ \text{nx} \right],\text{n}\in \text{N}$

(c) Signum function 

 

The function is defined as

$\text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{sgm}\left( \text{x} \right)$

$y=\text{sgn}\left( x \right)\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} -1 & x<0 \\ 0 & x=0 \\ 1 & x>0 \\ \end{array} \right.$

Q) Find the domain of the following : 

(a) ${{[\text{x}]}^{2}}-3\left[ \text{x} \right]+2\le 0$ 

(b) $4\left[ \text{x} \right]=\text{x}+\left\{ \text{x} \right\}$ 

Solution : 

(a) Given ${{[\text{x}]}^{2}}-3\left[ \text{x} \right]+2\le 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \left[ \text{x} \right]-1 \right)\left( \left[ \text{x} \right]-2 \right)\le 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }1\le \left[ \text{x} \right]\le 2$

Here the value of greatest integer function is $1\And 2$.

for value 2 , x can lie b/w $2\And 3$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Domain $\in \left[ 1,3 \right)$

(b) given the function, $4\left[ \text{x} \right]=\text{x}+\left\{ \text{x} \right\}$

but $\text{x}=\left[ \text{x} \right]+\left\{ \text{x} \right\}$

putting this

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} 4\left[ \text{x} \right] & ~=\left[ \text{x} \right]+\left\{ \text{x} \right\}+\left\{ \text{x} \right\} \\ 4\left[ \text{x} \right] & ~=\left[ \text{x} \right]+2\left\{ \text{x} \right\} \\ 3\left[ \text{x} \right] & ~=2\left\{ \text{x} \right\} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{x} \right\} & ~=\frac{3}{2}\left[ \text{x} \right]\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{x} \right]=\frac{2}{3}\left\{ \text{x} \right\} \\ \end{array}$

we know $0\le \left\{ \text{x} \right\}<1$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0\le \left[ \text{x} \right]<\frac{2}{3}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{x} \right]=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0\le \text{x}<1$ …………(i)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ but }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{x} \right\}=\frac{3}{2}\left[ \text{x} \right] \\ {} & \And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0\le \text{x}<1,\left[ \text{x} \right]=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \left\{ \text{x} \right\}=0. \\ \end{array}$ ………….(ii)

combining (i) & (ii)

The only value to satisfy the question is $\text{x}=0$

Q) Find the domain of the following : 

(a) ${{x}^{2}}-4x+\left[ x \right]+3=0$ (b) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }f\left( x \right)=\frac{\text{sin}\left[ x-2 \right]}{\left[ x-2 \right]\left[ x+3 \right]}$

Solution : (a) given that ${{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+\left[ \text{x} \right]+3=0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & {{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+\left( \text{x}-\left\{ \text{x} \right\} \right)+3=0 \\ \Rightarrow & {{\text{x}}^{2}}-3\text{x}+3=\left\{ \text{x} \right\} \\ \Rightarrow & 0\le {{\text{x}}^{2}}-3\text{x}+3<1 \\ \end{array}$

But ${{x}^{2}}-3x+3={{x}^{2}}-3x+\frac{9}{4}+3-\frac{9}{4}$

$={{\left( x-\frac{3}{2} \right)}^{2}}+\frac{3}{4}$

which is always greater than 0

solving now ${{x}^{2}}-3x+3<1$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & {{\text{x}}^{2}}-3\text{x}+2<0 \\ \Rightarrow & \left( \text{x}-2 \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)<0 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{x}\in \left( 1,2 \right) \\ \Rightarrow & \left[ \text{x} \right]=1 \\ \end{array}$

putting back the value in the original equation

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+4=0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(\text{x}-2)}^{2}}=0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=2 \\ \end{array}$

but from (i) $\text{x}\in \left( 1,2 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Thus equation has no solution.

(b) Given that $f\left( x \right)=\frac{\text{sin}\left[ x-2 \right]}{\left[ x-2 \right]\left[ x+3 \right]}$

there, there are 2 critical points $-3\And 2$ which divides the number line in 3 region.

  1. $-3\le \text{x}<2$

$\left[ \text{x}-2 \right]$ give negative values but there are no problem

but for $\left[ \text{x}+3 \right]$, if $\text{x}\in \left( -2,-3 \right]$

the function becomes invalid for these values as denominator becomes 0 .

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left[ -2,2 \right)$

  1. $x<-3$ in this region there is no problem as the function is valid for all values

$\Rightarrow \text{x}\in \left( -\infty ,-3 \right)$

  1. $x\ge 2$ for $\text{x}\in \left[ 2,3 \right)$, the function $\left[ \text{x}-2 \right]$ returns 0 , which makes the

function invalid but for $\text{x}\ge 3$ there is no problem.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left[ 3,\infty \right)$

combining (i), (ii) & (iii)

$\text{x}\in \left( -\infty ,-3 \right)\cup \left[ -2,2 \right)\cup \left[ 3,\infty \right)$

concepts.title: Finding the domain a given function
concepts.conceptBody:
Domain

Working Rule:

In order to find the domain of the function defined by $y=f\left( x ight)$, find the real values of $x$for

which $y$ is defined i.e. $ ext{y}$ is real. The set of all these values of $ ext{x}$ will be the domain.

Use the following information’s whichever is required.

  1. (a) $ ext{sin}x$ and $ ext{cos}x$ are defined for all real $x$.

(b) $ ext{tan}x$ and sec $x$ are not defined at odd multiples of $ rac{\pi }{2}$

(c) $ ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ cot}x$ and $ ext{cosec}x$ are not defined at multiples of $\pi $.

(d) $ egin{align} & -\,1\,\le \,\sin \,x\,\le \,1\,\,\, \ & -1\,\le \,\cos \,x\,\le \,1\,\,\, \ \end{align}$

$ egin{align} & -\,\infty \,<\, an \,x\,<\,\infty \, \ & -\,\infty \,<\,\cot \,x\,<\,\infty \, \ \end{align}$

$ egin{align} & \sec \,x\,\le \,-1\,or\,\sec \,x\,\ge \,1 \ & \cos ec\,x\,\le \,-1\,or\,\operatorname{cosec}\,x\,\ge \,1 \ \end{align}$

2. (a) $ ext{si}{{ ext{n}}^{-1}} ext{x}$ and $ ext{co}{{ ext{s}}^{-1}} ext{x}$ are defined if and only if $-1\le ext{x}\le 1$.

(b) $ ext{ta}{{ ext{n}}^{-1}} ext{x}$ and $ ext{co}{{ ext{t}}^{-1}}$ are defined for all real $ ext{x}$.

(c) $ ext{se}{{ ext{c}}^{-1}} ext{x}$ and $ ext{cose}{{ ext{c}}^{-1}} ext{x}$ are defined if and only if $ ext{x}\le -1$ or $ ext{x}\ge 1$.

$- rac{\pi }{2}\le ext{si}{{ ext{n}}^{-1}} ext{x}\le rac{\pi }{2} ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ }$ $0\,\le \,{{\cos }^{-1}}x\,\le \,\pi $

$- rac{\pi }{2}\,<\,{{ an }^{-1}}x\,<\, rac{\pi }{2}$ $0\,<\,{{\cot }^{-1}}x\,\,<\,\,\pi $

(d) $ ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ }- rac{\pi }{2}\le ext{cose}{{ ext{c}}^{-1}} ext{x}\le rac{\pi }{2} ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ }$ $0\,\,\le \,\,{{\sec }^{-1}}x\,\,\le \,\,\pi $

But $ ext{cosec}{{e}^{-1}}x e 0 ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ }$ But $ ext{se}{{ ext{c}}^{-1}}x e rac{\pi }{2}$

3. (a) $ ext{lo}{{ ext{g}}_{b}}a$ is defined if and only if $a>0,b>0$ and $b e 1\And a e 0$

(b) $ ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ lo}{{ ext{g}}_{b}}a>c\Leftrightarrow \left\{ egin{array}{*{35}{l}} a>{{b}^{c}}, ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ if }\!\!~\!\! ext{ }b>1 \ a<{{b}^{c}}, ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ if }\!\!~\!\! ext{ }b<1 \ \end{array} ight.$

(c) If $ ext{a}>0$, then ${{ ext{a}}^{ ext{x}}}$ is defined for all real $ ext{x}$.

4. For any function Denominator should never be zero.

5. For an algebraic function.

(a) Denominator should never be zero.

(b) an expression under even root should be $\geq 0$.

  1. Use sign scheme for a rational function if required.
  2. Find solution of Trigonometry inequality if required

Some useful tips to find domain 
(a) Domain of (f $\left( x ight)\pm g\left( x ight))$ $=$ Domain of $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)\cap $ Domain of $ ext{g}\left( ext{x} ight)$ 
(b) Domain of $\left( ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)\cdot ext{g}\left( ext{x} ight) ight)$ $=$ Domain of $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)\cap $ Domain of $ ext{g}\left( ext{x} ight)$ 
(c) Domain of $\left( rac{ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)}{ ext{g}\left( ext{x} ight)} ight)$ $=$ Domain of $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)\cap $ Domain of $ ext{g}\left( ext{x} ight)\left\{ ext{x}; ext{g}\left( ext{x} ight) e 0 ight\}$ 
(d) Domain of $\sqrt{ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)}$ $=$ Domain of $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)$ such that $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)\ge 0$ 
(e) Domain of $ ext{lo}{{ ext{g}}_{a}}f\left( x ight)$ $=$ Domain of $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)$ such that $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)>0$

concepts.title: Finding the domain and range of different functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Finding the domain and range of different functions

Q) If $\left[ x \right]$ denotes the integral part of $x$, find the domain of definition of the function.

$f\left( x \right)=\frac{\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x}{\sqrt{x-\left[ x \right]}}$

Solution : For $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be defined,

(i) $\text{x}-\left[ \text{x} \right]>0$ $\Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,x\,>\,[x]$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & \left[ \text{x} \right]<\text{x} \\ \Rightarrow & \text{x}\ne \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ an }\!\!~\!\!\text{ integer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & {} \\ \end{array}$

(ii) $\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}\text{x}$ should be defined,

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\le -1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\ge 1$

From (1) and (2), common values of $x$ are given by

$(-\infty <\text{x}<-1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }1<\text{x}<\infty )\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\notin \text{I}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Domain $=\text{R}-\left( \left( -1,1 \right)\cup \text{I} \right)$

Q) Find the domain of the function 

$f\left( x \right)=\text{log}\left\{ a{{x}^{3}}+\left( a+b \right){{x}^{2}}+\left( b+c \right)x+c \right\},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{b}^{2}}-4ac\left\langle 0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }a \right\rangle 0$

Solution : Given, $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{log}\left\{ \text{a}{{\text{x}}^{3}}+\left( \text{a}+\text{b} \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}+\left( \text{b}+\text{c} \right)\text{x}+\text{c} \right\}$ ………….(i)

For $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be defined,

$[\begin{align} & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,a{{x}^{2}}+\,(a+b){{x}^{2}}\,+\,(b+c)x+c\,>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(a{{x}^{2}}+b{{x}^{2}}+cx)+(a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c)>\,0 \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,x(a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c)+a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c>\,0 \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(x+1)\,(a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c)>\,0 \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,x+1>0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[\because {{b}^{2}}-4ac<0\,and\,\,a>0 \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\therefore a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c>\,0\,\,for\,all\,real\,x] \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,x>-1 \\ \end{align}$

Hence domain of $f=\left( -1,\infty \right)$

Q) Find the domain of definition of the following functions : 

(i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }f\left( x \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( {{x}^{2}}-4x+4 \right)$ (ii) $f\left( x \right)=\sqrt{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\frac{1}{2}}}\left( 2x-3 \right)}$

(iii) $f\left( x \right)=\sqrt{\frac{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x+2 \right)}{\left( x-3 \right)\left( x-4 \right)}}$ (iv) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left[ 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}-3 \right]$

([.] denotes the greatest integer function). 

Solution : 

(i) For $\text{f}$ ( $\text{x})$ to be defined $-1\le {{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+4\le 1$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-1\le {{(\text{x}-2)}^{2}}\le 1\Rightarrow \left| \text{x}-2 \right|\le 1\Rightarrow -1\le \text{x}-2\le 1\Rightarrow 1\le \text{x}\le 3$

Hence the domain of definition of $f\left( x \right)$ is the set $x\in \left[ 1,3 \right]$.

(ii) For $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be defined $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{1/2}}\left( 2\text{x}-3 \right)\ge 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{x}-3\le 1\Rightarrow \text{x}\le 2$ …... (1) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{a}}}\text{b}\ge 0 \right.$ when

Also $2\text{x}-3>0\Rightarrow \text{x}>\frac{3}{2}$ …....(2) 0 < a < 1, b ≤1]

Combining (1) and (2) we get the required values of $\text{x}$. Hence the domain of definition of

 

$\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is the $\text{set}\left( \frac{3}{2},2 \right]$

(iii) For $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be defined $\frac{\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}+2 \right)}{\left( \text{x}-3 \right)\left( \text{x}-4 \right)}\ge 0$ and $\text{x}\ne 3$, 4 .

By wayy-curve method the domain of definition of $f\left( x \right)$ is the set

$\text{x}\in \left( -\infty ,-2\left] \cup \right[1,3 \right)\cup \left( 4,\infty \right)$

(iv) For $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be defined $-1\le \left[ 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}-3 \right]\le 1$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-1\le 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}-3<2\Rightarrow 2\le 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}<5\Rightarrow 1\le {{\text{x}}^{2}}<5/2$

If ${{x}^{2}}\ge 1$ then $x\in \left( -\infty ,-1\left] \cup \right[1,\infty \right)$ ……..(1)

If ${{x}^{2}}<\frac{5}{2}$ then $x\in \left( -\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}},\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}} \right)$ ………(2)

Combining (1) and (2), $x\in \left( -\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}}-1 \right]\cup \left[ 1,\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}} \right)$
